Name: 6119G Clous de Paris
Description: The 6119G breathes fresh life into the Calatrava line with its refined reinterpretation of the iconic hobnail bezel. Launched in 2021, it blends contemporary proportions with classical detailing, featuring a sleek gray dial and small seconds for added elegance. Its clean aesthetic and slim profile are perfect for understated sophistication, while the hand-wound caliber nods to traditional watchmaking. A versatile piece for the modern collector who values heritage with a modern twist.
Specs: Movement: Manual Patek Caliber 30‑255 PS (65h power reserve); Case: 39 mm white gold, 8.1 mm thick; Dial: Gray, hobnail bezel; Strap: Black alligator; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: 5227G-010 Automatic Date
Description: Understated elegance meets old-world charm in the Calatrava 5227G. Crafted in 18k white gold, its clean ivory lacquer dial hides a secret: a hinged officer-style caseback, paying tribute to horological heritage. With automatic movement and date functionality, this 39 mm timepiece is ideal for the refined gentleman seeking discretion, comfort, and enduring Patek Philippe craftsmanship in one seamless design.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Patek Caliber 324 SC (~45h reserve); Case: 39 mm 18k white gold, hinged officer caseback; Dial: Ivory lacquer, gold dauphine hands, date aperture; Strap: Brown alligator leather; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: Weekly Calendar 5212A
Description: A rare expression of casual sophistication, the 5212A brings playful nuance to the Calatrava line. Housed in a stainless-steel case, its dial features a handwritten-style weekly calendar—one of Patek’s most charming complications. Released in 2019, it marries utility with uniqueness, driven by the automatic caliber 26‑330 S C J SE. An unconventional yet deeply collectible choice for connoisseurs who appreciate elegant innovation.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Patek Caliber 26‑330 S C J SE (weekly calendar, ~45h reserve); Case: 40 mm stainless steel; Dial: Opaline silver, day and week indicators (handwritten font); Strap: Calf leather, vintage brown; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: 5811/1G Blue Dial
Description: Arguably the most coveted luxury sports watch of the 21st century. A cultural icon and modern legend. A seamless continuation of one of the most celebrated designs in watchmaking history. The Nautilus 5811/1G builds on the legacy of the legendary 5711, reimagined in white gold with subtle refinements that honor its heritage. Drawing from Gérald Genta’s 1976 blueprint, this evolution retains the signature porthole-inspired case and horizontal embossing, now paired with a deeper, more radiant sunburst blue dial. Released in 2022 to mark the end of the steel era, the 5811 is more than a successor — it’s a statement of quiet luxury, exclusivity, and modern craftsmanship. Understated yet iconic, it signals a new chapter for Patek Philippe’s most coveted silhouette.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Patek Caliber 26‑330 S C; Case: 41 mm white gold, 8.2 mm thick; Dial: Sunburst blue with horizontal embossing, date at 3; Bracelet: Integrated white gold bracelet; Water Resistance: 120 m

Name: 5712/1A Moonphase
Description: The 5712/1A introduces asymmetry and complication to the Nautilus family. With its moonphase, power reserve, and analog date all arranged off-center, it feels artistically unbalanced—in the best way. Crafted in stainless steel, it maintains the Nautilus’s iconic silhouette while delivering extra mechanical intrigue. Loved for its nuanced dial layout and casual elegance, the 5712/1A is a cult favorite that blends sporty appeal with poetic complication.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Patek Caliber 240 PS IRM C LU (micro-rotor, ~48h reserve); Case: 40 mm stainless steel; Dial: Blue/gray, moonphase display, analog date, power reserve indicator; Bracelet: Integrated steel bracelet; Water Resistance: 60 m

Name: 5740/1G Perpetual Calendar
Description: Blending sporty aesthetics with grand complication, the 5740/1G is the first Nautilus to feature a perpetual calendar. Housed in 18k white gold, it delivers full calendar functionality while maintaining an ultra-thin profile—no small feat of engineering. Introduced in 2018, it became an instant flagship of the line, combining modern luxury with elite technical prowess. This is the Nautilus at its most complicated, elegant, and refined.
Specs: Movement: Ultra-thin Automatic Caliber 240 Q (perpetual calendar, moonphase, 48h); Case: 40 mm 18k white gold, 8.4 mm thick; Dial: Blue sunburst, day/date/month and moonphase subdials; Bracelet: Integrated white gold bracelet; Water Resistance: 60 m

Name: 5167A Steel
Description: The 5167A defines casual elegance in steel. Launched in 2007, this Aquanaut features a clean, embossed black dial and tropical rubber strap that screams versatility. Its 40 mm case hits the sweet spot for daily wear, while the automatic movement ensures reliability. The 5167A is beloved for its no-fuss design, contemporary sportiness, and touch of youthful energy—perfect for the collector who wants a luxury piece that’s truly wearable.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Patek Caliber 324 SC; Case: 40 mm stainless steel, ~8.5 mm thick; Dial: Black checkerboard pattern, applied numerals, date at 3; Strap: Black composite rubber with steel folding clasp; Water Resistance: 120 m

Name: 5168G-001
Description: Bolder in both presence and tone, the 5168G marks the 20th anniversary of the Aquanaut collection with a striking twist. Crafted in luxurious white gold, its oversized 42.2 mm case elevates the line’s sporty identity while maintaining its signature curvature and wearability. The gradient blue dial—shifting from deep navy to electric azure—adds modern vibrancy and depth, matched seamlessly by the color-matched rubber strap. Powered by the Caliber 324 S C, it balances rugged athleticism with refined execution. This is the Aquanaut for those who demand subtle flex, timeless silhouette, and uncompromising quality.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Patek Caliber 324 S C (~45h reserve); Case: 42.2 mm 18k white gold, 8.25 mm thick;Dial: Gradient blue embossed, gold applied numerals;Strap: Blue tropical rubber with white gold fold-over clasp;Water Resistance: 120 m

Name: 5968A Chronograph
Description: Launched in 2018, the 5968A is the Aquanaut’s answer to bold, modern sportiness. With a 60-minute flyback chronograph, bright orange accents, and an athletic steel case, it captures a new generation’s idea of fun luxury. Despite the flair, it retains Patek’s quality and finishing, making it both playful and prestigious. It’s not just a chronograph—it’s a statement.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Patek Caliber CH 28‑520 C (flyback chronograph, 45h reserve); Case: 42.2 mm stainless steel; Dial: Black embossed, orange chronograph hand and 60-min counter; Strap: Orange or black composite rubber strap; Water Resistance: 120 m

Name: 6300G Grandmaster Chime
Description: The Grandmaster Chime 6300 is a monumental creation—Patek’s most complicated wristwatch with 20 complications across two dials. First introduced in 2014 as ref. 5175, this masterpiece features a reversible case with chiming functions, perpetual calendar, dual time zones, and more. Made in minuscule quantities and often by special order, it is the pinnacle of haute horlogerie. Part sculpture, part symphony—there’s nothing else quite like it.
Specs: Movement: Hand-wound Patek Caliber 300 GS ALU 36‑750 QIS FUS IRM (1366 parts, 30h chiming reserve); Case: 47.7 mm, reversible double-dial (white gold); Complications: Grande & Petite Sonnerie, Minute Repeater, Alarm, Perpetual Calendar (four-digit year), Moonphase, Dual Time, Power Reserves, etc; Strap: Alligator leather

Name: 5303R Minute Repeater Tourbillon
Description: The 5303R is Patek Philippe’s boldest expression of mechanical artistry, combining a minute repeater and visible tourbillon in a fully openworked dial. Launched in 2020, it’s the first Patek repeater to display its striking mechanism on the dial side—letting wearers admire the chiming hammers and rotating tourbillon in real time. Its rose and white gold hybrid case adds dynamic contrast, while the hollowed lugs and engraved caseband elevate its sculptural presence. Built in extremely limited numbers and rarely seen publicly, it offers a rare glimpse into the maison’s inner workings. Both an acoustic marvel and a visual spectacle, the 5303R departs from the brand’s usual restraint, celebrating transparency, innovation, and high horology with unapologetic flair. A true collector’s piece that pushes tradition forward without losing its soul.
Specs: Movement: Manual Patek Caliber R TO 27 PS (tourbillon, minute repeater, ~40h); Case: 42 mm rose gold & white gold hybrid, hollowed lugs; Dial: Skeleton-style, showcasing hammers and tourbillon; Strap: Black alligator; Other: Slide lever for repeater, engraved caseband

Name: 5270J
Description: The 5270J is a connoisseur’s favorite, blending perpetual calendar and chronograph into a timeless 18k yellow gold case. Part of a legendary lineage that began in the mid-20th century, this model continues Patek's tradition of mechanical poetry and functional clarity. Its opaline dial balances the complex displays with classic grace. Whether on the wrist or under a loupe, it reveals a depth of character that defines grand complication at its most elegant.
Specs: Movement: Manual Patek Caliber CH 29‑535 PS Q (perpetual calendar, column-wheel chronograph; 65h); Case: 41 mm 18k yellow gold; Dial: Opaline white, day/month windows, date and moonphase at 6, 30-min and seconds subdials; Strap: Brown alligator; Other: Leap year & day/night indicators

Name: 43175 Perpetual Calendar
Description: The 43175 is a celebration of minimalist haute horlogerie. With its ultra-thin automatic movement and refined perpetual calendar layout, it distills elegance into every detail. The opaline dial, baton markers, and polished gold case make it a study in balanced design. Though technically complex, it whispers rather than shouts—a piece for those who recognize true craftsmanship without ostentation. Boutique-only, it reflects Vacheron's quiet mastery.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Calibre 1120 QP/1 (~40h reserve, Geneva Seal; 276 parts, 36 jewels, 2.75Hz, 4.05mm thick); Case: 41 mm 18K pink gold, 8.9mm thick; Dial: Silver-toned opaline or blue sunburst with day/date/month/subdials, moonphase; Strap: Dark blue or brown alligator leather with 18K pink gold folding clasp; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: 43172 Perpetual Calendar
Description: Balancing heritage and complexity, the 43172 (Ref: 43172/000P-B190 platinum) embodies the timeless spirit of the Patrimony collection. Housed in a 41 mm platinum case, it features a classic opaline dial displaying day, date, month, leap year, and moonphase—all powered by the ultra-thin Caliber 1120 QP. With just 4.05 mm of movement thickness and Geneva Seal finishing, it represents the pinnacle of Vacheron’s understated mastery. Elegant, collectible, and boutique-exclusive.
Specs: Movement: Automatic VC Caliber 1120 QP (~40h reserve, Geneva Seal);Case: 41 mm platinum, ~8.96 mm thick;Dial: Opaline with calendar subdials and moonphase;Strap: Black alligator;Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: Retrograde Day-Date
Description: The Retrograde Day-Date spices up the Patrimony line with twin fan-shaped arcs for day and date, creating a visually distinctive dial without sacrificing elegance. Encased in warm pink gold, it strikes a balance between classical cues and technical playfulness. Whether you’re drawn to its artistic layout or mechanical novelty, it’s a refreshing take on the modern dress watch—both cerebral and beautiful.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 2460 R31R7 (retrograde day and date, ~40h reserve); Case: 42.5 mm 18k pink gold; Dial: Silvered opaline, retrograde day scale at top, retrograde date at bottom; Strap: Brown alligator leather; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: 4500V Overseas
Description: Effortlessly blending ruggedness with refinement, the Overseas 41 mm is a steel sports watch with haute horlogerie credentials. Its sunburst blue dial is framed by a notched bezel, and the quick-release strap system adds versatile flair. Water-resistant to 150 meters and Geneva Seal certified, it’s a rare watch that can handle both cocktails and coastline with equal ease.
Specs: Movement: Automatic VC Caliber 5100 (60h reserve, Geneva Hallmark); Case: 41 mm stainless steel, screw-down crown; Dial: Sunburst blue, luminous hour markers, date at 3; Bracelet/Strap: Interchangeable (steel bracelet + blue rubber & leather straps); Water Resistance: 150 m

Name: Overseas Chronograph
Description: The Overseas Chronograph is a full-size 42.5 mm statement piece, housing an in-house column-wheel chronograph and finished to Geneva Seal standards. With three crisp subdials and a date tucked between 4 and 5, it balances complexity with legibility. Whether worn on steel, leather, or rubber, it’s a high-performance tool with dress watch polish—a rare hybrid that truly excels.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 5200 (column-wheel chronograph, 52h reserve, Geneva Seal); Case: 42.5 mm stainless steel, 13.7 mm thick; Dial: Silver or blue, 3 chronograph subdials (30-min, 12-hr, small seconds), date between 4 & 5; Bracelet/Strap: Interchangeable steel bracelet and straps; Water Resistance: 150 m

Name: 6000V Overseas Tourbillon
Description: A contemporary masterpiece from the Overseas collection, the Vacheron Constantin Overseas Tourbillon (Ref. 6000V/210T-H032) blends the sport-luxury aesthetic with haute horlogerie excellence. Housed in a 42.5 mm grade 5 titanium case and measuring only 10.39 mm thick, it features a vibrant blue sunburst dial with a prominent tourbillon aperture at 6 o’clock. The watch is equipped with an interchangeable strap system and arrives with a titanium bracelet, blue rubber, and leather straps for versatile wear. Lightweight, elegant, and technically accomplished, it exemplifies modern refinement. Boutique-exclusive and produced in limited quantities.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 2160 (ultra-thin tourbillon, 80h reserve, Geneva Seal); Case: 42.5 mm grade 5 titanium, 10.39 mm thick; Dial: Blue sunburst with tourbillon aperture at 6; Strap: Interchangeable system (titanium bracelet, blue rubber, and leather); Water Resistance: 50 m

Name: American 1921
Description: Inspired by a 1920s driving watch, the American 1921 is delightfully unconventional. With a cushion case and diagonal dial layout, it was made for reading time with one hand on the wheel. Manual-winding and vintage in soul, it’s a love letter to the Art Deco age with just the right amount of eccentricity. History meets horology in the most elegant way.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber 4400 AS (65h reserve, Geneva Seal); Case: 40 mm 18k white or rose gold, cushion-shaped; Dial: Off-white/cream, black Arabic numerals rotated 45°, small seconds at 4 o’clock; Strap: Brown or burgundy alligator; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: Cornes de Vache 1955
Description: A beloved modern classic, the Cornes de Vache 1955 Ref. 5000H/000P-B058 revives Vacheron Constantin’s first water-resistant chronograph from the mid-20th century. Named after its signature “cow horn” lugs, this 38.5 mm piece features a hand-wound Caliber 1142 with column-wheel chronograph architecture. The opaline dial with blued hands, vintage subdials, and tachymeter scale oozes heritage charm. A refined balance between elegance and functionality, it stands as one of the most coveted neo-vintage chronographs on the market.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber 1142 (column-wheel chronograph, ~48h reserve, Geneva Seal); Case: 38.5 mm platinum or stainless steel; Dial: Opaline with two subdials (30-min chronograph and small seconds), blued chronograph hands, tachymeter scale around edge; Strap: Black or brown alligator; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: Triple Calendrier 1942
Description: The Triple Calendrier 1942 Ref. 3110V/000A-B426 offers a faithful reissue of Vacheron’s 1940s triple calendar, now with a modern manual-wind Caliber 4400 QC and 40 mm stainless steel case. With tear-drop lugs, red-tipped date hand, and two-tone silver dial, it merges 20th-century design with 21st-century precision in limited numbers.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber 4400 QC (calendar module, ~65h reserve); Case: 40 mm stainless steel; Dial: Two-tone silver, blue Arabic numerals, day and month in windows, date indicated by red-tipped hand around the dial; Strap: Brown calfskin; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: Copernicus Celestial Spheres
Description: A poetic marvel, the Copernicus Celestial Spheres 2460 RT honors heliocentrism with a mesmerizing enamel and engraved dial depicting planetary orbits. Time is read from rotating peripheral indicators, allowing Copernicus’s sun-centered universe to shine. Housed in a 43 mm 18K gold case, it contains the automatic Caliber 2460 RT. An astronomical tribute merging fine art and horology, limited in number and unmatched in artistry.
Specs: Movement: Automatic in-house (Caliber 2460 RT) with rotating celestial indicator; Case: 43 mm 18k gold; Dial: Grand Feu enamel and hand-engraving depicting planetary orbits and constellations; Display: Hours and minutes via peripheral indicators (to not obscure art); Others: Each piece hallmarked and unique artwork

Name: Les Aérostiers
Description: The Métiers d’Art Les Aérostiers celebrates 18th-century ballooning with exquisite plique-à-jour enamel hot air balloons in 3D relief. Each 40 mm gold case holds a unique dial with engraving and guilloché, time shown via wandering discs to preserve the scenery. With extremely limited production, each is a wearable sculpture powered by an in-house automatic caliber.
Specs: Movement: Automatic in-house caliber with unconventional time display (e.g., sliding digital hours, minutes on disc); Case: ~40 mm 18k gold; Dial: Handcrafted gold balloon in translucent enamel, finely engraved landscapes; Display: Artistic (no traditional hands; uses apertures or peripheral scales); Other: Each model limited to a few pieces

Name: 6007A Scorpio Celestial
Description: A masterpiece from Vacheron Constantin’s Métiers d’Art collection, this 39mm white-gold timepiece celebrates Scorpio through a meticulously hand‑guilloché zodiac motif and its constellation. Brilliant-cut diamonds mark key stars, while rich blue enamel and precisely set baguette-cut sapphires (≈3.87ct across bezel, lugs, crown & buckle) frame the dial. The geometric ribbed white‑gold indexes and sword‑shaped hands echo the artistry beneath. Each dial’s creation—selecting design, engraving, varnish, gem‑setting—reflects an uncompromising commitment to tradition and high crafts. Boutique‑exclusive and engineered as wearable haute horlogerie art.
Specs: Movement: Calibre 2160 – a self‑winding ultra‑thin tourbillon movement (188 components, 5.65mm thick), beating at 2.5Hz (18,000vph), offering an 80‑hour power reserve via a solid 22K gold peripheral rotor. Central hours and minutes hands are complemented by a small‑seconds indicator on the Maltese‑cross‑shaped tourbillon carriage. Finishing includes perlage, Côtes de Genève bridges, chamfered screws, and Geneva Seal certification.

Name: Celestia Astronomical
Description: A one-of-a-kind double-sided wristwatch boasting 23 astronomical complications (sunrise/sunset, equation of time, perpetual calendar, celestial chart, moon phases, tide level, etc.). Released in 2017 as a bespoke piece, it required several years of development. Housed in a 45 mm white gold case only 13.6 mm thick – a triumph of miniaturization. The Celestia exemplifies VC’s pinnacle bespoke watchmaking in the Cabinotiers department.
Specs: Movement: In-house Caliber 3600 (manual, 3 weeks power reserve with 6 barrels); Case: 45 mm 18k white gold, double-sided dials; Complications: 23 functions incl. perpetual calendar, equation of time, times of sunrise/sunset, sky chart, sidereal time, indications for solstices and equinoxes, etc.; Strap: Black alligator; Unique piece (2017)

Name: Symphonia Grande Sonnerie
Description: Vacheron’s first ever grande sonnerie wristwatch (also with petite sonnerie and minute repeater) delivered through Cabinotiers in 2017. This unique piece chimes the hours and quarters automatically (or on demand) with exceptional acoustic engineering. Despite housing one of the most complex striking mechanisms, the watch maintains classic styling in a 45 mm gold case. Represents an extraordinary feat of high watchmaking and chiming innovation. Piece-unique.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber 1860 (gran&petite sonnerie + minute repeater, ~72h reserve); Case: 45 mm 18k white gold; Functions: Automatic hour/quarter striking (selectable silence), on-demand minute repeater, power reserve indicators for striking and movement; Dial: Silvered with small seconds and power reserve subdials; Unique piece (Cabinotiers commission)

Name: “Phoenix” Minute Repeater Tourbillon
Description: An example of a one-off Cabinotiers creation: a highly complicated watch (tourbillon + minute repeater) with extraordinary artisanal decoration. The “Phoenix” features a hand-engraved case depicting phoenix motifs in relief, often accented with enamel or gem-setting. Such a piece demonstrates VC’s ability to create singular works blending technical complication (e.g., flying tourbillon, repeater) with exquisite métiers d’art. Unique commission, not part of regular production.
Specs: Movement: One-off manual caliber integrating minute repeater and tourbillon; Case: ~44–45 mm 18k gold, fully hand-engraved with phoenix design; Dial: Possibly skeletonized or enamelled to complement theme (open view of tourbillon); Functions: Hours, minutes, tourbillon regulator, minute repeater (cathedral gongs); Note: Unique personalized creation by Les Cabinotiers

Name: “Jumbo” Extra-Thin 16202ST
Description: An icon reborn for its 50th anniversary, the 16202ST is the spiritual successor to the original 1972 Royal Oak. Crafted in stainless steel with the legendary octagonal bezel and “Petite Tapisserie” blue dial, this 39 mm masterpiece honors Gérald Genta’s vision in purest form. Now powered by the next-generation Caliber 7121, it remains impossibly slim and effortlessly timeless—every detail whispering prestige, heritage, and scarcity.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 7121 (extra-thin, 55h reserve); Case: 39 mm stainless steel, 8.1 mm thick; Dial: Blue Petite Tapisserie pattern, white gold hour markers, date at 3; Bracelet: Integrated steel bracelet with AP folding clasp; Water Resistance: 50 m

Name: Selfwinding Chronograph 41
Description: Bold yet balanced, the 41 mm Royal Oak Chronograph blends technical depth with unmistakable Royal Oak design. Framed by the signature octagonal bezel, the dial reveals a rich Grande Tapisserie texture, punctuated by subdials and a date aperture. Inside beats a column-wheel chronograph, marrying mechanical precision with daily practicality. It's a commanding presence for those who seek complexity wrapped in classic steel sculpture.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 2385 (column-wheel chronograph, 40h reserve); Case: 41 mm stainless steel, 11 mm thick; Dial: Grande Tapisserie pattern (blue/black/white), 3 chronograph subdials, date at 4:30; Bracelet: Integrated steel; Water Resistance: 50 m

Name: Perpetual Calendar
Description: Elegance meets eternity in the Royal Oak Perpetual Calendar. This 41 mm marvel unites Haute Horlogerie and iconic design, offering day, date, month, moonphase, leap year, and even week of the year—artfully arranged on a richly textured dial. Powered by the ultra-thin Caliber 5134, it is impossibly slim for its mechanical ambition. A poetic expression of time’s passage, housed within a design that redefined modern watchmaking.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 5134 (ultra-thin perpetual calendar, 40h reserve); Case: 41 mm in steel or 18k gold, 9.5 mm thick; Dial: Tapisserie with four subdials (day, date, month with leap year, moonphase) + week indicator scale on outer chapter; Bracelet: Integrated matching metal bracelet; Water Resistance: 20 m

Name: Offshore “The Beast” (1993)
Description: Audemars Piguet’s original rebel, the Offshore “Beast” shattered norms when it roared onto wrists in 1993. Its 42 mm steel case, rubber-clad pushers, and Méga Tapisserie dial redefined boldness in high horology. Dubbed “The Beast” for its aggressive size and attitude, it marked the dawn of a new era—bigger, louder, and unapologetically audacious. Now a legend, it remains a cornerstone of contemporary sport-luxury.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 2126/2840 (based on JLC 889, modular chronograph, ~42h reserve); Case: 42 mm stainless steel, ~15 mm thick, rubber-clad screw-down crown & pushers; Dial: Blue Méga Tapisserie, 3 subdials (chronograph), date at 3; Strap: Stainless steel bracelet (early models) or rubber/leather; Water Resistance: 100 m

Name: Offshore Diver
Description: Where form meets function at 300 meters, the Offshore Diver is the embodiment of utility clad in Royal Oak armor. Its 42 mm case frames a vivid Méga Tapisserie dial, with an internal rotating bezel operated via an additional crown—designed for precise underwater timing. Crafted in steel with color-matched rubber straps, it transforms tool-watch functionality into a modern design statement, uncompromising in both aesthetics and performance.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 3120 (time and date, 60h reserve) in later models Caliber 4308; Case: 42 mm stainless steel, sapphire display back, screw-down crowns; Dial: Méga Tapisserie pattern, prominent lumed markers, rotating inner bezel for dive timing; Strap: Rubber strap with AP pin buckle (color-matched to dial); Water Resistance: 300 m

Name: Offshore Chronograph
Description: The evolution of AP’s most daring line, the modern Offshore Chronograph channels raw energy into refined construction. Its 42 mm case hosts a dramatic dial layered with subdials, bold indices, and that signature Méga Tapisserie texture. The rubber-clad crown and pushers amplify its tactile toughness, while the movement within delivers robust performance. Whether forged in steel, ceramic, or carbon, this is power dressing for the modern iconoclast.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 3126/3840 (base cal 3120 + Dubois-Depraz chronograph module, ~50h); Case: 42 mm stainless steel (or other materials), rubber-clad crown and pushers; Dial: Méga Tapisserie, 3 chronograph subdials, date at 3 (magnified); Strap: Rubber or leather with tang buckle; Water Resistance: 100 m

Name: Concept (2002)
Description: Audemars Piguet’s boldest vision debuted in 2002: the Royal Oak Concept. Designed for the 30th anniversary of the Royal Oak, this titanium marvel fused mechanical ingenuity with futuristic form. With its sculpted asymmetrical case, exposed bridges, and groundbreaking dynamograph torque indicator, it redefined what a mechanical watch could express. Revered by collectors, the 2002 Concept is more than a timepiece—it’s a modernist manifesto in titanium.
Specs: Movement: Hand-wound Caliber 2896 (tourbillon, 70h reserve, dynamograph torque indicator, function selector); Case: 44 mm titanium, unique shape with protruding crown guard; Dial: Openworked, tourbillon at 9 o’clock, linear power reserve gauge at 12, function selector at 6; Strap: Kevlar/Nylon strap (original); Water Resistance: 50 m

Name: Concept Flying Tourbillon GMT
Description: A study in architectural tension and lightness, the Concept Flying Tourbillon GMT (Ref. 26589IO circa 2018) combines a titanium frame with a black ceramic bezel and 10-day power reserve. The skeletonized dial reveals a flying tourbillon and 3D GMT globe with day/night indicator—each element layered like a futuristic sculpture. Visceral, sharp-edged, and unapologetically avant-garde, this is AP’s design lab on full display.
Specs: Movement: Hand-wound Caliber 2954 (flying tourbillon, second time zone with day/night indicator, ~10d power reserve); Case: 44 mm titanium, black ceramic octagonal bezel; Dial: Openworked, tourbillon at 9, GMT globe and 24-hour disc at 3, skeletal bridges; Strap: Black rubber strap; Water Resistance: 100 m

Name: Concept “Black Panther” Flying Tourbillon
Description: A 2021 limited edition (250 pieces) co-designed with Marvel. 42 mm titanium case with a sculpted Black Panther character in hand-painted white gold on the dial. Equipped with a flying tourbillon at 6 o’clock. The design merges comic book art with haute horlogerie, featuring purple accents and futuristic aesthetics. Caused a sensation upon release for its unexpected collaboration and highly detailed craftsmanship. All pieces sold out immediately.
Specs: Movement: Hand-wound Caliber 2965 (flying tourbillon, 72h reserve); Case: 42 mm titanium, black ceramic bezel; Dial: 3D hand-sculpted and painted Black Panther figure in gold, flying tourbillon at 6, no traditional hour markers (hands tell time against the figure); Strap: Purple rubber strap; Water Resistance: 50 m

Name: Tribute Small Seconds
Description: A modern homage to the original 1930s Reverso, featuring a clean Art Deco design. Rectangular 45.6 × 27.4 mm case in stainless steel, manual wind movement. Front dial is a sunray blue (or other colors in variants) with small seconds at 6. The case flips over (empty metal back for personalization or display of movement in some versions). Embodies JLC’s classic elegance and the Reverso’s sporting origin (designed for polo players).
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber 822/2 (~45h reserve); Case: 45.6 × 27.4 mm stainless steel, reversible; Dial: Blue sunray (Tribute model), applied indices, small seconds subdial at 6; Strap: Fagliano Collection blue leather (for Tribute edition); Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: DuoFace Classic
Description: Iconic double-sided Reverso offering two dials for dual time zones. The front dial shows the home time (classic design, often silver guilloché), while the case flips to reveal a second dial (usually black or contrasting) with a second time zone and day/night indicator. Housed in a medium or large rectangular case. Manual movement driving both faces. A signature JLC innovation combining elegance and practicality. Current production model in the Reverso Classic Duoface line.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber 854A/2 (dual time, 2 dial outputs, ~42h reserve); Case: ~47 × 28 mm stainless steel (Large size example), reversible; Dial: Front – silvered with central guilloché, black numerals; Reverse – black with Clous de Paris texture, second timezone hours/minutes and 24h indicator; Strap: Black or brown leather; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: Gyrotourbillon 2
Description: A haute complication Reverso introduced in 2008, showcasing JLC’s multi-axis “Gyrotourbillon.” The oversized Reverso case (55 × 36 mm in platinum) houses a spherical tourbillon that rotates in multiple axes for improved timekeeping precision. The dial features a partial skeletonization, with a separate subdial for the time and a 50-hour power reserve indicator. This remarkable piece marries the Reverso’s reversible case concept with one of watchmaking’s most mesmerizing complications. Extremely limited production.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber 174 (double-axis Gyrotourbillon, 50h reserve); Case: 55 × 36 mm platinum, reversible (Gyrotourbillon visible on one side, time display on the other); Dial: Skeletonized elements, main dial with blued hands for hours/minutes, seconds on tourbillon cage, power reserve indicator; Strap: Black alligator; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: Moon
Description: A refined dress watch featuring a moonphase and date in JLC’s Master Ultra Thin line. 39 mm stainless steel case with a clean silvered dial. The moonphase display and pointer date are positioned at 6 o’clock. Powered by a slim automatic movement. Balances complication and elegance in an ultra-thin profile that slides easily under a cuff. One of JLC’s most popular contemporary dress models. Current production.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 925/1 (moonphase, date, ~43h reserve); Case: 39 mm stainless steel, 9.9 mm thick; Dial: Silvered sunray, applied indices, aperture at 6 with moonphase and crescent date hand; Strap: Black alligator with deployant clasp; Water Resistance: 50 m

Name: Perpetual Calendar
Description: An ultra-thin perpetual calendar housed in a 39 mm Master case (examples in stainless steel or gold). Displays day, date, month, moonphase, and year without the need for adjustment until 2100. Despite the complexity, the watch is only about 9.2 mm thick, showcasing JLC’s engineering. The dial layout is balanced with three subdials and a year window. Renowned as one of the most value-driven perpetual calendars in high horology. Current model (various metals).
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 868/1 (perpetual calendar, 38h reserve); Case: 39 mm stainless steel (or pink gold), ~9.2 mm thick; Dial: White or silvered, day at 9, month and year at 12, date and moonphase at 3, leap year indicator; Strap: Brown or black alligator; Water Resistance: 50 m

Name: Tourbillon
Description: A classic round dress watch integrating a tourbillon in an ultra-thin profile. 40 mm case (often pink gold) with a prominent tourbillon visible at 6 o’clock on the dial, acting as the seconds indicator. The overall design is understated, highlighting the rotating tourbillon cage. Automatic winding via a peripheral rotor in some versions to maintain slimness. Demonstrates JLC’s prowess in fine finishing and complication integration in a slim case. Current high-end offering in the Master line.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 978 (tourbillon, 45h reserve, COSC chronometer rated); Case: 40 mm 18k rose gold, ~11.3 mm thick; Dial: Silvered with sunray brush, cutout at 6 o’clock for the one-minute tourbillon (blued seconds hand on cage); Strap: Brown alligator leather; Water Resistance: 50 m

Name: Mariner Memovox
Description: A diving-inspired alarm watch paying tribute to the 1968 Memovox Polaris. 42 mm stainless steel case, blue gradient dial. Equipped with a mechanical alarm (Memovox) that sounds underwater – a rare complication for divers. Has two crowns (one for time/alarm winding and setting, one for inner rotating dive bezel) plus an alarm on/off indicator. 300 m water resistant with a transparent caseback that lets one see the hammer mechanism. A unique blend of vintage charm and modern specification. Current model (limited availability).
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 956 (alarm complication, 45h reserve); Case: 42 mm stainless steel, 15.6 mm thick, screw-down crowns; Dial: Blue gradient, luminous indices, alarm disk at center (triangle marker), inner rotating bezel for dive timing; Strap: Stainless steel bracelet; Water Resistance: 300 m

Name: Date
Description: A contemporary take on the retro Polaris design, without the alarm complication. 42 mm steel case, black or blue dial with mixed finishing (grained and sunray segments) and large Arabic numerals at cardinal points. Internal rotating bezel for timing. Date window at 3 o’clock. 200 m water resistance. It’s a versatile sport-elegant watch that nods to JLC’s skin-diver heritage while offering everyday functionality. Current production.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 899A/1 (~38h reserve); Case: 42 mm stainless steel, 13.9 mm thick, dual crowns; Dial: Black with vintage-style creamy lume, internal rotating bezel (operated by 2 o’clock crown), date at 3; Strap: Black calfskin leather (textured); Water Resistance: 200 m

Name: Chronograph WT
Description: A bold Polaris model combining chronograph and world-time functions. 44 mm titanium case for lightness, sporting a busy yet legible dial: outer rotating city disk for 24 time zones, inner 24-hour ring, and two chronograph subdials. Despite complications, retains 100 m water resistance. Black and white dial accents for clarity, with orange touches on the second time zone indicators. A true traveler’s sports watch that showcases JLC’s technical range. Limited edition model in the Polaris line (limited to 250 pieces).
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 752 (chronograph with world time, 65h reserve); Case: 44 mm titanium, exhibition back; Dial: Black with world cities ring on flange, 24-hour disk, 30-min and 12-hr chrono subdials, central seconds, luminous markers; Strap: Brown calfskin; Water Resistance: 100 m

Name: Chronographe
Description: Part of JLC’s innovative Duomètre series, this 42 mm watch contains twin power trains (“dual-wing”) – one dedicated to timekeeping, one to the chronograph – both regulated by the same escapement. This ensures using the chronograph doesn’t affect timekeeping precision. The dial (eggshell white) features jumping seconds (1/6th second resolution), a chronograph minute and hour counter, and a unique twin power reserve display. A showcase of technical creativity and traditional style. Limited production, introduced 2007.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber 380 (dual-wing: separate barrels for time and chrono, 50h reserve each); Case: 42 mm 18k yellow gold (or white gold), approx. 13.5 mm thick; Dial: Ivory grained, twin coaxial seconds (foudroyante & running) at 6, 60-min and 12-hr chrono counters at 3 & 9, small hour/minute dial at 2, dual power reserves; Strap: Brown alligator; Water Resistance: 50 m

Name: Quantième Lunaire
Description: A dual-wing model focusing on calendar and astronomical display. It separates power for timekeeping and complications to optimize accuracy. 40.5 mm case in gold. The dial shows off-center hours/minutes, moonphase and date on the right, and a unique seconds with jumping hand (1/6th sec) on the left with a sweeping seconds beneath. Combines technical ingenuity with classical moonphase beauty. Introduced 2010, produced in limited numbers.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber 381 (dual-wing: one gear train for time, one for moonphase/date; 50h per barrel); Case: 40.5 mm 18k pink gold; Dial: Opaline silver, hour/minute subdial at 2, moonphase and date subdial at 4, jumping 1/6th second hand + sweeping seconds on left side; Strap: Brown alligator; Water Resistance: 50 m

Name: Sphérotourbillon
Description: The Duomètre series’ pinnacle: a multi-axis tourbillon combined with dual-wing architecture. The Sphérotourbillon (introduced 2012) features a tourbillon that rotates in two axes (inclined at 20° and spinning once per minute, and rotating fully every 30 seconds on second axis), eliminating gravity effects in most positions. Housed in a 42 mm case (often platinum or rose gold). The dial displays time, a second timezone (subdial at 6), and a second hand that can be stopped (for precise setting) without halting the balance. A marvel of engineering and fine finishing, blending complication with practicality. Very limited production.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber 382 (dual-wing with Sphérotourbillon, 50h reserve); Case: 42 mm 18k rose gold or platinum; Dial: Grained ecru, main time at 12, second time zone subdial at 6, aperture at 9 for the 3D tourbillon, power reserve at 4; Strap: Black or brown alligator; Other: Second hacking mechanism stops seconds hand without stopping tourbillon (for synchronization)

Name: Lange 1 (Yellow Gold)
Description: A horological icon born in 1994, the Lange 1 is the very soul of modern Saxon watchmaking. With its asymmetrical dial, oversized date, and twin barrels, it declares its defiance of symmetry while embracing absolute precision. Encased in 18k yellow gold, this 38.5 mm sculpture of time is both regal and restrained — a quietly confident masterstroke that helped re-establish Glashütte’s prestige.
Specs: Movement: Manual Lange L121.1 (double barrel, big date, power reserve indicator, 72h); Case: 38.5 mm 18k yellow gold; Dial: Silvered, off-center hours/minutes, big date at 2, small seconds and power reserve at 5; Strap: Brown alligator; Other: Stop-seconds mechanism

Name: Lange 1 Time Zone
Description: Lange’s definitive travel companion. The Time Zone retains the architectural poise of the Lange 1 while adding a second timezone, day/night indicator, and 24-city ring to chart the world. Encased in pink gold, its dial is a symphony of balance, function, and subtle depth — a cosmopolitan complication for the true global aesthete.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber L031.1 (dual time, 72h); Case: 41.9 mm 18k pink gold; Dial: Solid silver, main time at left, second time zone at 5 with day/night, big date at 2, city disc for 24 time zones; Strap: Brown alligator

Name: Grand Lange 1 Moon Phase
Description: A larger stage for poetic function. The Grand Lange 1 Moon Phase preserves the off-center harmony of its smaller sibling while introducing a beautifully integrated moonphase into the hour subdial. The argenté dial, pink or white gold case, and finely detailed lunar disc combine to deliver astronomical elegance at full scale.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber L095.3 (moonphase, 72h reserve); Case: 40.5 mm 18k white gold; Dial: Argenté, off-center time with integrated moonphase disc, big date at 2, sub-seconds at 7; Strap: Black alligator

Name: Digital Display
Description: Groundbreaking mechanical digital watch introduced in 2009. Hours and minutes displayed by jumping numerals in large apertures; small seconds and power reserve on dial. 42 mm case in white gold. Constant-force escapement ensures precise minute jumps. A bold blend of avant-garde display and traditional finishing. Ongoing production (recently updated movement in 2022).
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber L043.1 (remontoir constant-force, jumping hours/minutes, 36h); Case: 42 mm 18k white gold; Dial: Black/silver two-tone, digital hour and minute windows, small seconds at 6, power reserve at 12; Strap: Black alligator

Name: Striking Time
Description: Time, made audible. The Zeitwerk Striking Time marries digital display with a visible chiming mechanism — two polished hammers ringing out the hours and quarters in crisp tonal clarity. A masterpiece of mechanical orchestration, it transforms passing time into sound and spectacle.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber L043.2 (jumping time with quarter-hour chimes, 36h); Case: 44.2 mm 18k white gold; Dial: Rhodium-plated, time displayed digitally, two polished steel hammers at 5 and 7 (strike on gong around dial); Strap: Black alligator

Name: Minute Repeater
Description: An acoustic marvel of our time. This platinum Zeitwerk is the world’s first mechanical watch to chime its digital display using a decimal minute repeater. Each strike — in tens and minutes — echoes with precision and poetry. It is a sublime confluence of engineering genius and horological audacity.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber L043.5 (decimal minute repeater with digital display, 32h); Case: 44.2 mm platinum; Dial: Black, digital hours/minutes, small seconds at 6, repeater hammers at 4 and 8 (chime ten-minute intervals and minutes); Strap: Black alligator

Name: Up/Down
Description: A connoisseur’s chronograph. The Datograph Up/Down is revered for its hand-finished flyback chronograph movement and perfectly poised dial: large date, twin subdials, and a power reserve indicator (Up/Down). Its platinum case and black dial command presence, while its caliber L951.6 remains one of the most admired chronograph movements ever made.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber L951.6 (flyback chronograph, big date, power reserve 60h); Case: 41 mm platinum; Dial: Black, outsize date at 12, 30-min chrono and small seconds subdials, UP/DOWN power reserve at 6; Strap: Black alligator

Name: Perpetual
Description: Two titans in one. The Datograph Perpetual unites flyback chronograph and perpetual calendar in a 41 mm white gold case. Its slate grey dial choreographs moonphases, leap years, and chronograph counters in perfect harmony. A triumph of complexity, symmetry, and Saxon finesse.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber L952.1 (chrono + perpetual calendar, 36h); Case: 41 mm 18k white gold; Dial: Grey, big date at 12, day/leap year at 9, month at 3, moonphase and day-night at 6, 30-min and running seconds subdials; Strap: Black alligator

Name: Perpetual Tourbillon
Description: The summit of A. Lange & Söhne’s ingenuity. Tourbillon, chronograph, and perpetual calendar — all concealed beneath a dignified black dial, with the tourbillon revealed only on the movement side. Limited to just 100 pieces, this 41.5 mm platinum rarity represents the apex of technical watchmaking and discreet grandeur.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber L952.2 (chrono, perpetual calendar, one-minute tourbillon, 50h); Case: 41.5 mm platinum; Dial: Black, layout similar to Datograph Perpetual, tourbillon visible on movement side; Strap: Black alligator; Limited: 100 pieces

Name: Thin
Description: Minimalism, perfected. The Saxonia Thin strips time to its essence — two hands, pure silver dial, and a case so slender it disappears under any cuff. Yet beneath its modest façade lies a hand-finished movement and a statement of restraint and refinement. Quiet, but never ordinary.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber L093.1 (thickness 2.9 mm, 72h reserve); Case: 40 mm 18k rose gold (5.9 mm thick); Dial: Solid silver, minimalist (no seconds or date), applied baton markers; Strap: Brown alligator

Name: Moon Phase
Description: The moon, reimagined with Saxon clarity. This 40 mm dress watch pairs an outsized date at 12 with a luminous moonphase at 6. Its automatic movement, gold rotor, and classic layout make it a perfect expression of mechanical elegance. The lunar complication, framed in restraint.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber L086.5 (big date, moonphase, 72h); Case: 40 mm 18k white gold; Dial: Argenté, Lange outsize date at 12, moonphase and seconds subdial at 6; Strap: Black alligator

Name: Annual Calendar
Description: Understated brilliance. The Saxonia Annual Calendar offers seamless month-to-month adjustment with the charm of a moonphase and big date. At 38.5 mm, it balances legibility and grace — ideal for collectors who appreciate practical complication cloaked in timeless design.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber L085.1 Sax-o-Mat (annual calendar, zero-reset seconds, 46h); Case: 38.5 mm 18k pink gold; Dial: Silver, big date at 12, day and month windows at 10 & 2, moonphase and small seconds at 6; Strap: Brown alligator

Name: Excellence Panorama Date Moon Phase
Description: A masterclass in Germanic restraint, the Senator Excellence Panorama Date Moon Phase marries clarity and complication in 40mm steel. Its Panorama big‑date and moonphase are harmoniously balanced and framed by a finely textured dial. Powered by the in‑house Caliber 36‑04 with a 100‑hour reserve, each watch passes Glashütte Original’s 24‑day Excellence testing—ensuring chronometric precision far beyond standard chronometer limits. A quietly confident expression of Saxon watchmaking, ideal for collectors who prize precision as much as poise.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 36-04 (100h reserve); Case: 40 mm steel; Dial: Lacquered white, Panorama big date at 4, moonphase at 10; Strap: Black alligator; Water Resistance: 50 m

Name: Chronometer
Description: An ode to marine chronometry, the Glashütte Original Chronometer channels the spirit of high-seas navigation into a 42 mm white gold frame. Certified for chronometric precision by the German Calibration Service, it features a stop-seconds reset and minute detent — horological tools designed for pinpoint accuracy. The grain-textured silver dial, Roman numerals, and symmetrically arranged power reserve and small seconds evoke classical symmetry, while the in-house Caliber 58-01 beats within. It’s a seafarer’s soul encased in Saxon gold — a timekeeper built for precision, not spectacle.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber 58-01 (stop-seconds reset, 45h); Case: 42 mm white gold; Dial: Grained silver, big date at 3, power reserve at 12, small seconds at 6; Strap: Blue alligator

Name: Cosmopolite
Description: A technical triumph for the jet age, the Cosmopolite is Glashütte Original’s answer to global modernity. Its 44 mm white gold case houses the Caliber 89-02, capable of tracking 37 time zones — including rare half- and quarter-hour offsets. Home and local times are read at a glance, complemented by DST indicators and the brand’s iconic Panorama Date. This world timer is more than an instrument; it’s a horological passport for those whose lives cross continents and calendars. Bold, brilliant, and breathtakingly intelligent.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 89-02 (world time, 72h); Case: 44 mm white gold; Dial: Lacquered silver, apertures for city codes, day/night, home/local time subdials, Panorama Date; Strap: Black alligator

Name: PanoMaticLunar
Description: A signature of Glashütte Original’s Pano collection, the PanoMaticLunar weaves moonphase and Panorama date into an asymmetric dial aesthetic crafted with rare elegance. At 40mm steel, the off‑center time subdial, sweeping small seconds, and gentle lunar aperture form a visual poem. Inside, the automatic Caliber 90‑02 powers up to 42 hours and reveals a historically inspired three‑quarter plate and swan‑neck regulator through the display caseback. It’s subtle German precision cloaked in poetic design—a collectible born of clarity and nuance.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 90-02 (moonphase, date, 42h); Case: 40 mm steel; Dial: Blue, off-center time at left, Panorama Date at 4, moonphase at 2; Strap: Blue Louisiana alligator

Name: PanoReserve
Description: Minimalism, measured. The PanoReserve strips timekeeping to its conceptual core, presenting hours, seconds, and power reserve across a galvanized silver dial like an architect’s sketch. Housed in 40 mm of red gold, its hand-wound Caliber 65-01 delivers tactile engagement and visual harmony. The off-center layout, signature to Glashütte Original, is elevated by warm metal tones and refined typography. This is restrained opulence — not shouting, simply showing.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber 65-01 (42h reserve); Case: 40 mm 18k red gold; Dial: Silver, off-center hour/min, small seconds at 7, power reserve arc at 2, Panorama Date at 4; Strap: Brown alligator

Name: PanoInverse
Description: A mechanical mirror turned outward, the PanoInverse transforms Glashütte Original’s movement architecture into dial-side art. At 42 mm in polished steel, it invites the eye into its inverted Caliber 66-06 — hand-engraved balance cock, blued screws, and twin swan-neck regulators proudly on display. Time is told off-center, suspended above this kinetic sculpture. The effect is theatrical, precise, and unmistakably German: a stage where chronometry and craft perform in harmony.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber 66-06 (inverted movement design, 41h); Case: 42 mm steel; Dial: Open movement plate with hand engravings, blued screws, off-center time subdial; Strap: Black alligator

Name: 1969 Limited Edition
Description: Vintage spirit, modern steel. Limited to 69 pieces, the SeaQ 1969 resurrects Glashütte Original’s first diver with period-accurate fidelity — from its creamy lume to the “25 Rubis” dial print. The 39.5 mm case houses the automatic Caliber 39-11, rated to 200 m and paired with either nylon or Tropic-style rubber. Each detail is deliberate, down to the retro Arabic numerals and red depth rating. This isn’t retro-chic — it’s history revived, water-sealed.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 39-11 (40h); Case: 39.5 mm steel, unidirectional bezel; Dial: Matte black, creamy luminous numerals, date at 3; Strap: Tropic-style rubber or nylon; Water Resistance: 200 m

Name: Panorama Date (Blue)
Description: Function meets finesse in the SeaQ Panorama Date — a modern diver with Saxon sensibilities. The 43.2 mm steel case boasts 300 m of water resistance and a ceramic blue bezel, but it’s the sunray dial and signature big date that elevate this from utilitarian to exceptional. Beneath, the Caliber 36-13 delivers 100 hours of autonomy. Whether desk-diving or deep-sea dwelling, this is the rare tool watch that keeps its tie on.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 36-13 (100h, Panorama Date); Case: 43.2 mm steel, ceramic bezel, screw-down crown; Dial: Sunray blue, applied numerals/indexes with lume, big date at 4; Strap: Stainless steel bracelet or blue rubber; Water Resistance: 300 m

Name: (Reed Green)
Description: A verdant twist on Glashütte Original’s classic diver, the SeaQ Reed Green wraps vintage inspiration in nature’s hue. The 39.5 mm steel case, rated to 200 m, encircles a matte green dial with bold Arabic numerals and date at 3. Inside, the Caliber 39-11 ticks reliably with 40 hours of reserve. This is not camouflage — it’s contrast, making a statement in the sea of black-dial dive watches.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 39-11 (40h); Case: 39.5 mm steel, unidirectional green bezel; Dial: Reed green, large luminous numerals and indices, date at 3; Strap: Green nylon strap or steel bracelet; Water Resistance: 200 m

Name: Black
Description: Stealth mode, Saxon style. The SeaQ Black brings Glashütte’s diving pedigree into a sleek, classic profile: 39.5 mm stainless steel, matte black dial, luminous sword hands. With a screw-down crown and 200 m resistance, it’s ready for adventure — yet refined enough for the boardroom. The Caliber 39-11 within may be hidden, but its heritage echoes in every tick. Understated. Underwater. Undeniably GO.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 39-11 (40h);Case: 39.5 mm steel, unidirectional black bezel;Dial: Matte black, luminous sword hands and bold indices, date at 3;Strap: Black rubber strap or steel bracelet;Water Resistance: 200 m

Name: Panorama Date Blue
Description: Glashütte Original channels the depth of Saxony’s lakes into this commanding blue diver. A fusion of German engineering and maritime grace, the SeaQ Panorama Date elevates tool-watch utility to refined art. Its deep dial shimmer, impeccable finishing, and panoramic calendar reflect a contemporary expression of GO’s historic marine chronometers—crafted not just to endure the elements, but to do so with quiet, Teutonic dignity.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 36-13 (100h, silicon spring);Case: 43.2 mm steel, unidirectional ceramic blue bezel;Dial: Sunburst blue with applied markers, big Panorama Date at 4;Strap: Blue rubber strap or steel bracelet;Water Resistance: 300 m

Name: Reed Green
Description: Sporting a verdant hue pulled from dense forests surrounding Glashütte, this SeaQ edition exudes purpose with poise. Compact, robust, and unmistakably German, it fuses functional purity with rich color and detail. It’s an adventurer’s timepiece, yet dignified enough to share a wrist with a suit. A nod to GO’s precision roots, now reimagined for the modern explorer.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 39-11 (40h);Case: 39.5 mm steel, unidirectional green bezel;Dial: Reed green, large luminous numerals and indices, date at 3;Strap: Green nylon strap or steel bracelet;Water Resistance: 200 m

Name: Chronomètre Souverain
Description: Elegant 40 mm dress watch known for its chronometric performance. Power reserve at 3, small seconds at 7, clean dial (often silver guilloché or Havana brown). Rose gold movement. Manual wind with twin barrels for stable timekeeping. Embodies Journe’s classical side. Still part of core collection.
Specs: Movement: Manual FP Journe Caliber 1304 (18k rose gold, twin barrels, ~56h); Case: 40 mm or 42 mm, available in platinum or red gold; Dial: Off-center sub-seconds, power reserve 56h indicator; Strap: Alligator leather

Name: Chronomètre Bleu
Description: Cult-favorite variant of the CS: 39 mm tantalum case, vivid blue lacquer dial. Same caliber 1304 inside. Sought for its unique metal and dial color combination and limited production. No date, just superb finishing and a distinctive modern look. Extremely high demand among collectors.
Specs: Movement: Caliber 1304, rose gold bridges; Case: 39 mm tantalum; Dial: Electric blue chrome dial, small seconds at 7, power reserve at 3; Strap: Brown or blue leather

Name: Chronomètre Optimum
Description: Journe’s ultimate chronometer, incorporating a double wheel escapement and remontoire for constant force. 40 mm case, time-only with small seconds and 70h power reserve indicator on back. Delivers extremely high precision. Very limited production, showcases François-Paul Journe’s technical pinnacle in timekeeping.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber 1510 (double direct impulse escapement, 42h constant force remontoire, ~70h total); Case: 40 mm platinum or red gold; Dial: Off-center hours/min, small seconds at 9; Display Back: Power reserve visible on back; Strap: Crocodile leather

Name: Réserve de Marche
Description: One of Journe’s early automatic models (Octa line) with a long power reserve ~120h. 40 mm case. Features an off-center time display, large date, and power reserve indicator. F.P. Journe’s first automatic caliber (Octa 1300) known for fitting complications in a thin design. A practical yet uniquely styled piece. No longer produced (replaced by newer Octa models).
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 1300.1 (5-day power, 18k rose gold rotor); Case: 40 mm platinum or gold; Dial: Off-center hours/min, small seconds at 4:30, power reserve at 10:30, large date at 11; Strap: Leather strap

Name: Lune
Description: An Octa automatic featuring a moonphase and large date. 40 mm case. Asymmetrical dial with time, small seconds, and power reserve on left, and moonphase with date on right. 120h power reserve from the same thin Octa caliber. Combines everyday practicality with poetic moonphase. Discontinued model in current form (Journe updates dials periodically).
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 1300.3 (5-day, moonphase module); Case: 40 mm rose gold or platinum; Dial: Time subdial at 9, small seconds at 5, big date and moonphase at 1–2, reserve at 10; Strap: Alligator

Name: Calendrier (Annual Calendar)
Description: Journe’s interpretation of an annual calendar. Uses Octa automatic movement modified for full calendar (day, date, month) requiring adjustment once per year (February). 40 mm case. Unconventional dial with retrograde date arc across the top and day/month windows. Limited production model showcasing practicality in Journe’s elegant style. (No longer in current catalog; produced mid-2000s.)
Specs: Movement: Automatic Caliber 1300.2 (annual calendar module, ~120h); Case: 40 mm platinum or gold; Dial: Retrograde date arc from 8 to 4, day window at 9, month window at 3, time subdial at 12, seconds at 4; Strap: Leather

Name: Souverain -1999
Description: F.P. Journe’s first production model (1999): a tourbillon with remontoire (constant-force device) and deadbeat seconds – the first ever wristwatch to combine these. 38 mm or 40 mm case in platinum or gold. Dial with tourbillon visible at 9 and seconds, and power reserve at 12. This early series (“Souscription” and subsequent) established Journe’s reputation. Discontinued, succeeded by updated models.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber 1498 (18k rose gold, one-minute tourbillon, 42h, remontoire for constant force, deadbeat seconds); Case: 38 mm or 40 mm; Dial: Off-center time at 3, tourbillon aperture at 9, seconds on tourbillon, power reserve at 12; Strap: Alligator

Name: (Dead-Seconds)
Description: The second generation (circa 2004) Tourbillon Souverain incorporating Journe’s “seconde morte” (deadbeat seconds) visibly on dial. Largely similar to original but with slight dial changes (central seconds subdial) and movement improvements. 40 mm case typically. Demonstrates Journe’s continued refinement of his tourbillon concept. Discontinued 2019 (replaced by Vertical).
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber 1403 (tourbillon with remontoire & dead seconds, ~42h); Case: 40 mm gold or platinum; Dial: Time subdial at 3, tourbillon at 9, dead seconds indicated centrally or at 6 (depending on edition), power reserve; Strap: Leather

Name: Vertical
Description: 20th anniversary edition of Journe’s tourbillon (2019) – features a vertically oriented tourbillon cage. Limited to 20 pieces in tantalum with bright blue dial ring. Also includes remontoire and dead seconds. Unique aesthetics with tourbillon visible through cut-out in dial and sapphire top on the case. Marks a celebratory evolution of Journe’s hallmark complication. All sold out immediately.
Specs: Movement: Manual Caliber 1519 (vertical tourbillon, constant force, deadbeat seconds, 80h); Case: 42 mm tantalum; Dial: Blue lacquered ring, cutaway center displaying vertical tourbillon mechanism; Strap: Blue alligator; Limited: 20 pieces

Name: (Invention 1)
Description: Greubel Forsey’s first invention, debuted in 2004: an innovative Double Tourbillon mechanism with one tourbillon cage inclined at 30° inside a larger 4-minute tourbillon cage. Housed in a 43.5 mm case (typically in gold). This construction averages out gravity effects in multiple positions, dramatically improving chronometric performance. Only a few examples were made early on, laying the foundation for the brand’s philosophy of extreme chronometry and hand-finishing.
Specs: Movement: Manual Greubel Forsey caliber (Double Tourbillon 30° mechanism, 1 inner cage rotating in 60s, 1 outer cage in 4min, 72h+ reserve); Case: ~43.5 mm 18k gold, with lateral display window on some editions; Dial: Silvered (partial open-work), off-center time display, power reserve indicator; Strap: Hand-sewn alligator; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: Technique
Description: An openworked execution of the Double Tourbillon 30°, unveiled 2009. Features a fully skeletonized dial movement under a sapphire crystal, showcasing every mechanism in 3D. Four fast-rotating barrels provide 120h (5 days) of power
Specs: Movement: Manual Greubel Forsey Double Tourbillon 30° (inclined inner 1-minute tourbillon inside 4-minute outer tourbillon, 120h reserve via four barrels); Case: 47.5 mm (various: WG/RG/PT, Titanium, Sapphire, Ceramic); Dial: Fully openworked, indicating hours, minutes, small seconds, 72h chronometric power reserve; Strap: Hand-stitched alligator; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: Asymétrique
Description: Limited to 22 pieces (2013–2014), this version reimagined the Double Tourbillon 30° in an asymmetrical case design
Specs: Movement: Manual Greubel Forsey Double Tourbillon 30° (twin-axis tourbillon with 1min/4min rotations, 72h reserve); Case: 43.5 mm asymmetrical, 18k red or white gold, bulging sapphire window on case side; Dial: Multi-level, time subdial at 1 o’clock, power reserve at 2, exposed tourbillon at 8; Strap: Black hand-sewn alligator; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: (Contemporain)
Description: Greubel Forsey’s second invention, first presented in 2007: a single tourbillon inclined at 25° and making a rapid revolution in 24 seconds. This high-speed inclined tourbillon averages out timing errors in more positions. Early models like the Tourbillon 24 Secondes Asymétrique used asymmetric cases, while the Contemporain (2012) introduced a round 43.5 mm case (often in platinum with a blue dial). These pieces display a dramatic elevated tourbillon at 8 o’clock and have a 72h power reserve. A significant evolution in GF’s pursuit of chronometry
Specs: Movement: Manual Greubel Forsey Tourbillon 24s (inclined 25° fast tourbillon, 24s per rotation, 72h reserve via twin barrels); Case: 43.5 mm platinum or gold; Dial: Blue enamel or multi-level silvered, off-center hours/minutes, 24s tourbillon at 8 o’clock with inclined cage, power reserve indicator; Strap: Hand-sewn alligator; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: Vision
Description: A notable variant that won the Aiguille d’Or (Grand Prix) in 2015. The Tourbillon 24 Secondes Vision placed the fast inclined tourbillon into a relatively slim 43 mm round case with a domed sapphire on the caseback to accommodate the cage’s height. Platinum with a subdued dial, it was lauded for blending Greubel Forsey’s technical virtuosity with elegant, wearable design. A clear, understated enamel dial hides the inclined tourbillon which is visible through the sapphire rear dome. Limited to 22 pieces in platinum.
Specs: Movement: Manual Greubel Forsey Tourbillon 24 Secondes (fast 24s inclined tourbillon, 72h reserve); Case: 43 mm platinum, 13.65 mm thick with curved sapphire protrusion on caseback; Dial: White enamel, blued steel hands, tourbillon visible only via rear display dome; Strap: Black alligator; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: “Architecture
Description: A contemporary marvel (released 2022) showcasing a fully modernized design. The Architecture presents the 24-second inclined tourbillon in a new 47 mm convex titanium case with panoramic sapphire openings in the case band. The movement is a concentric, skyscraper-like construction with layered bridges and skeletonized structures, visible from all angles. It indicates hours and minutes on a minimalist sapphire ring. This piece embodies GF’s futuristic approach, pushing the limits of aesthetics and mechanics. Extremely limited production.
Specs: Movement: Manual Greubel Forsey Tourbillon 24 Secondes (inclined high-speed tourbillon, 90h reserve); Case: 47 mm titanium, convex profile with lateral sapphire windows; Dial: Sapphire chapter ring for hours/minutes, multi-level movement architecture with suspended gear bridges, tourbillon at 6 o’clock; Strap: Textured rubber or alligator; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: Balancier S (Convexe)
Description: Greubel Forsey’s take on a luxury sports watch, introduced 2020. The Balancier S features a large, inclined balance wheel (12.6 mm) prominently visible on the dial, inside a 44 mm lightweight titanium case with an ergonomic “convex” profile and integrated rubber strap. It offers 100 m water resistance – unusual for GF – and a 72h power reserve. The design includes a curved sapphire crystal and sloping case sides for a bold, modern aesthetic. It marked a new, sportier direction for the brand while preserving GF’s high finishing standards.
Specs: Movement: Manual Greubel Forsey Caliber with 30° inclined balance (large balance wheel, 72h reserve); Case: 44 mm titanium, convex shape, curved sapphire, screw-down crown; Dial: Open architecture with inclined balance at 7, small seconds and power reserve at 2, luminescent indices; Strap: Integrated textured rubber; Water Resistance: 100 m

Name: Double Balancier Convexe
Description: Introduced 2022, this model showcases two inclined balance wheels with differential coupling, in a bold 42.5 mm convex titanium case. The dual balances (each at a 30° tilt) oscillate independently, averaging out positional errors and improving timekeeping. A lateral sapphire window wraps around the case for viewing the movement sides. Limited to 88 pieces (2022–2026), it demonstrates Greubel Forsey’s pursuit of chronometric performance in a contemporary, sport-oriented design.
Specs: Movement: Manual Greubel Forsey Double Balancier (two inclined 30° balance wheels with spherical differential, 72h reserve); Case: 42.5 mm titanium, convex profile, display back and side sapphire ring; Dial: Openworked, suspended gear train bridges, two visible balances, power reserve indicator at 2, small seconds at 8; Strap: Black rubber or textured leather; Water Resistance: 100 m

Name: Balancier S²
Description: A 2022 update to the Balancier S, refining the design with a smaller 41.5 mm convex case and cleaner dial details. The Balancier Convexe S² retains the signature inclined large balance at 6 o’clock but opts for a more open dial with modern finishing (micro-blasted and polished elements). Produced in limited editions (e.g. 88 pieces in titanium with blue accents), it represents a more streamlined, slightly more wearable evolution of Greubel Forsey’s sport line. Same 72h hand-wound movement inside, with superior finishing on display.
Specs: Movement: Manual Greubel Forsey Balancier (30° inclined large balance, 72h power reserve); Case: 41.5 mm titanium, convex shape, crown guard; Dial: Skeletonized with matte and glossy black bridges, large 12.6 mm balance at 6, power reserve at 3, hour/minute ring; Strap: Textured rubber or nylon; Water Resistance: 100 m

Name: (Original)
Description: One of Greubel Forsey’s most complex creations, the Quantième Perpétuel à Équation combines a perpetual calendar with an equation-of-time display and inclined tourbillon. Introduced at SIHH 2015, it features GF’s patented Mechanical Computer system to synchronize all calendar indications. The dial (often silvered) shows day, large date, month, leap year, and power reserve, plus hours, minutes, seconds. On the back, an extensive astronomical display shows the equation of time and seasons. Housed in a 43.5 mm gold case, it’s an ultra-complicated watch made in very small numbers (2–3 pieces per year).
Specs: Movement: Automatic Greubel Forsey QP à Équation (perpetual calendar + 24s inclined tourbillon + equation of time, 72h reserve); Case: 43.5 mm 18k white gold; Dial: Multi-tier, displays for hours, minutes, small seconds, day, big date, month, leap year, power reserve 72h; Reverse: Transparent back with equation of time scale, month, season, solstice/equinox indicators, and year; Strap: Hand-sewn alligator; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: ,(Red Gold, “Chocolate” Dial)
Description: A 2020 edition of the Greubel Forsey QP à Équation in a 5N red gold case with a rich chocolate-brown multi-level dial. This warmer-toned execution presents a different aesthetic for the QP à Équation, while maintaining the same mechanical complexity: synchronized displays for date, day, month, leap year, and equation of time, plus a 24-second tourbillon. Only a handful of these were made, each showcasing GF’s top-notch finishing (hand frosting, anglage, and black polishing) paired with the more casual elegance of a brown dial/strap combination.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Greubel Forsey Caliber QP à Équation (7th “Invention” with Mechanical Computer, inclined tourbillon, 72h); Case: 43.5 mm 5N red gold; Dial: Multi-level in chocolate brown and silver tones, apertures for day, big date, month, leap year, power reserve, sector for seconds; Strap: Brown hand-stitched leather; Water Resistance: 30 m

Name: “Millésime” Edition
Description: A special Millésime edition of the QP à Équation introduced in 2017 (with a rhodium-plated dial). Greubel Forsey periodically releases Millésime versions as showcase pieces – this one continued the legacy of the 2015 original by adding unique finishing touches and sometimes incremental enhancements. It retains the same functionality: bi-directional perpetual calendar (easily adjustable forward/backward) with equation of time and tourbillon. Essentially a rare variant for collectors, underscoring GF’s constant drive to refine even its most complicated watches.
Specs: Movement: Automatic Greubel Forsey QP à Équation; Case: 43.5 mm precious metal (often white gold); Dial: Varied finish (e.g. rhodium-plated) with comprehensive calendar displays, inclined tourbillon visible between 7 and 8 o’clock; Strap: Black or brown alligator; Limitation: Millésime editions are extremely limited (often one per year)

Name: (1953 “Big Crown”)
Description: The original Rolex Submariner, launched 1953 – the first divers’ wristwatch waterproof to 100 m. Early references like the 6538 featured an oversized crown (no crown guards) and a black rotating bezel with 15-minute markings, setting the template for dive watches. Famously worn by James Bond (Sean Connery). Powered by a chronometer-rated automatic movement. An icon of tool watch design, now highly collectible; it established the Submariner as the archetype of dive watches.
Specs: Movement: Rolex Caliber 1030 (early automatic, COSC certified); Case: 38 mm stainless steel, no crown guards, bidirectional timing bezel; Dial: Black gloss gilt, luminous radium indices and Mercedes hands, no date; Strap: Riveted Oyster steel bracelet or NATO strap; Water Resistance: 100 m (first to achieve in 1953)

Name: “Hulk” 116610LV
Description: A modern classic Submariner variant featuring a green dial and green Cerachrom bezel, nicknamed the “Hulk.” Introduced in 2010 and produced until 2020, it was the first Submariner Date with a green dial (the 50th anniversary “Kermit” of 2003 had a green bezel but black dial). 40 mm stainless steel Super Case with broader lugs and a solid link bracelet. Its distinctive look and relatively limited production run have made it highly sought-after since discontinuation.
Specs: Movement: Rolex Caliber 3135 (automatic, 48h reserve, COSC); Case: 40 mm 904L stainless steel, unidirectional ceramic bezel insert (green), Triplock screw-down crown; Dial: Metallic sunburst green, white gold hands and indices with Chromalight lume, date at 3 with Cyclops lens; Bracelet: Oyster bracelet with Glidelock clasp; Water Resistance: 300 m

Name: “No Date” 124060
Description: The current-generation Submariner (no-date version) introduced in 2020. 41 mm Oystersteel case with slightly revised proportions (slimmer lugs) and an upgraded movement. Classic black dial and bezel. It continues the Submariner legacy as a robust professional dive watch for everyday wear. Equipped with Rolex’s latest caliber 3230 and featuring the Oyster bracelet with Glidelock extension. Official retail price around A$13,700 (2023).
Specs: Movement: Rolex Caliber 3230 (automatic, ~70h reserve, Chronergy escapement, COSC Superlative Chronometer); Case: 41 mm Oystersteel, unidirectional Cerachrom bezel (black), Triplock crown; Dial: Black, white gold applied hour markers with Chromalight lume; Bracelet: Oyster steel with Glidelock extension clasp; Water Resistance: 300 m

Name: Cosmograph “Paul Newman” ref. 6239
Description: The most iconic vintage Rolex Daytona, referred to as the “Paul Newman” due to the actor Paul Newman famously wearing one. Reference 6239 (produced mid-1960s) with the distinctive exotic dial: art-deco style fonts, square-tipped subdial markers, and contrasting black/cream coloration. Originally a slow seller, these now extremely rare variants became auction sensations decades later. Manual-wind chronograph movement by Valjoux. An absolute grail for collectors – one such piece (Paul Newman’s own) fetched a record price in 2017
Specs: Movement: Rolex Caliber 722 (based on Valjoux 72, 18,000 vph, 17j manual chronograph); Case: 37 mm stainless steel, pump pushers, domed acrylic crystal; Dial: “Exotic” white dial with black subdials, red outer minutes track, art-deco numerals on subdials; Strap: Vintage-style leather or Oyster bracelet; Water Resistance: 50–100 m (approx, not originally rated for deep diving)

Name: ref. 16520 “Zenith”
Description: The first automatic Rolex Daytona, introduced in 1988 and powered by a modified Zenith El Primero movement. Reference 16520 in stainless steel, 40 mm case. It featured a gloss black or white dial with contrasting rings and an outer seconds track. Its launch transformed the Daytona from a slow seller into a waitlisted icon. Produced until 2000, this model is highly collectible – especially early “floating” dial variants and the final series with engraved rehaut. It represents the bridge between vintage manual Daytonas and modern in-house models.
Specs: Movement: Rolex Caliber 4030 (modified Zenith El Primero, 28,800 vph reduced to 36,000, 52h reserve, COSC); Case: 40 mm stainless steel, screw-down pushers, sapphire crystal; Dial: Black or white, applied hour markers with lume, 3 subdials for 30-min, 12-hr, running seconds; Bracelet: Oyster steel bracelet; Water Resistance: 100 m

Name: ref. 116500LN (Ceramic Bezel)
Description: The current steel Daytona model (launched 2016) featuring a black Cerachrom ceramic bezel. Reference 116500LN, 40 mm Oystersteel case. Available with white or black dial, it became one of the most in-demand luxury watches worldwide, with multi-year waitlists. Powered by Rolex’s in-house chronograph caliber 4130 with 72h power reserve. It carries on the Daytona legacy with modern upgrades (ceramic bezel for scratch resistance, Chromalight blue lume) while preserving the classic 3-subdial layout and 100 m water resistance.
Specs: Movement: Rolex Caliber 4130 (in-house automatic chronograph, vertical clutch & column wheel, 72h, COSC Superlative); Case: 40 mm Oystersteel, black Cerachrom bezel with tachymetric scale, screw-down pushers; Dial: White or black lacquer, luminous indices, 3 subdials (chronograph hours, minutes, small seconds); Bracelet: Oyster steel with Oysterlock clasp; Water Resistance: 100 m

Name: “Big Jubilee” ref. 4467
Description: The original Rolex Datejust, introduced in 1945 to celebrate Rolex’s 40th anniversary. Reference 4467 in 18k yellow gold, 36 mm Oyster case. It was the first wristwatch to feature a date window that automatically jumped at midnight, hence “Date-Just.” It also debuted the five-piece link Jubilee bracelet. Characterized by a coin-edge bezel (predecessor to the fluted bezel) and a champagne dial. This landmark model set the stage for one of Rolex’s most enduring and versatile designs – the Datejust, marketed as a symbol of prestige and precision.
Specs: Movement: Rolex Caliber 710 (automatic “Perpetual” with date mechanism, COSC for later versions); Case: 36 mm 18k yellow gold, coin-edge bezel, acrylic crystal; Dial: Champagne, golden applied indices, date window at 3 (initially without Cyclops magnifier, which was added in 1950s); Bracelet: Jubilee 18k gold (introduced with this model); Water Resistance: 50 m (Oyster case)

Name: 36 Two-Tone (ref. 16233)
Description: A classic example of the Datejust in its quintessential two-tone configuration. Reference 16233 (circa 1988–2004) features a 36 mm stainless steel case with 18k yellow gold fluted bezel and crown, and a steel/gold Jubilee bracelet. Typically seen with a champagne dial and bar markers. Powered by Rolex’s Caliber 3135 with quickset date. This era of Datejust was ubiquitous as a luxury status watch, combining durability with an elegant, versatile style – often considered the archetype of the modern Datejust.
Specs: Movement: Rolex Caliber 3135 (automatic, 28,800 vph, ~48h reserve, COSC); Case: 36 mm stainless steel with 18k yellow gold fluted bezel, sapphire crystal; Dial: Champagne or other options, applied indices or Roman numerals, date at 3 with Cyclops magnifier; Bracelet: Two-tone Jubilee (SS and 18k gold center links); Water Resistance: 100 m

Name: Datejust 41 (ref. 126334)
Description: A contemporary Datejust in the larger 41 mm size, introduced with the Datejust II (2009) and refined as Datejust 41 in 2016. Reference 126334 is a popular configuration: stainless steel case with 18k white gold fluted bezel (Rolex calls this White Rolesor), on an Oyster or Jubilee bracelet. Often paired with a blue or silver sunray dial. Houses the modern Caliber 3235 with 70h power and Chronergy escapement. This model retains the Datejust’s classic looks while offering an updated size and movement for modern tastes. Current production.
Specs: Movement: Rolex Caliber 3235 (automatic, ~70h reserve, Chronergy escapement, COSC + Rolex Superlative spec); Case: 41 mm Oystersteel, 18k white gold fluted bezel; Dial: Sunburst blue (option), white baton hour markers with Chromalight, date at 3 with Cyclops; Bracelet: Oyster or Jubilee in Oystersteel; Water Resistance: 100 m

Name: GMT-Master II “Fat Lady” ref. 16760
Description: The first GMT-Master II model, introduced 1983. Nicknamed “Fat Lady” for its thicker case profile (to accommodate the independently adjustable hour hand mechanism) and broader GMT bezel. 40 mm steel case with crown guards. Came with the red/black “Coke” bezel insert and a gloss black dial. This reference debuted the quickset 24-hour hand, allowing the GMT hand to be set separately – a major functional upgrade. Produced until 1988, it paved the way for all modern dual-time Rolexes. A transitional but important reference now appreciated by collectors.
